## Summary
- avoid reparsing the full accumulated assistant stream for ordinary plain text deltas in the embedded subscriber
- keep full reply-directive parsing for `text_end`, phase-aware replies, silent tokens, media/reply/audio directives, and streams whose prior raw text was already cleaned

Relates to https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw/issues/86599.

## Verification
- `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.handlers.messages.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.emits-block-replies-text-end-does-not.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.suppresses-commentary-phase-output.test.ts src/agents/embedded-agent-subscribe.subscribe-embedded-agent-session.subscribeembeddedagentsession.test.ts --reporter=dot` - 4 files / 96 tests passed

## Real behavior proof
Behavior addressed: Plain assistant text deltas no longer force full accumulated reply-directive parsing on every chunk while directive-bearing, final, and phase-aware paths keep the canonical full parse.

target.title: fix(browser): isolate Chrome MCP pending attach aborts
target.body:
## Summary

Fixes #88304.

- keep Chrome MCP cached-session attach cancellation scoped to the caller that cancelled
- track active waiters while a shared attach is still pending or waiting for `session.ready`
- tear down the attach only when the last waiter cancels, evict cancelled pending entries before a fresh caller can join them, and evict cached sessions before awaited close cleanup

## Motivation / root cause

`getSession()` shared one pending Chrome MCP attach per profile/cache key, but created that shared pending promise with the first caller's `AbortSignal`. If that first caller cancelled while another caller was also waiting for the same Chrome MCP session, the shared pending attach could reject with the first caller's abort reason and fail the unrelated caller.

The fix gives the shared attach its own abort controller and treats each caller as a waiter. Caller aborts release that waiter. The shared attach is only aborted or closed when no waiters remain, and last-waiter close cleanup removes the session from the cache before awaiting close so a fresh caller starts a new attach instead of reusing a handle being torn down.

## Real behavior proof

Behavior addressed: one cancelled Chrome MCP caller no longer cancels another caller waiting on the same shared pending attach; all-cancelled attaches are still cleaned up; fresh callers do not reuse a session that is already closing after last-waiter cancellation.

Real environment tested: macOS local checkout with the Chrome MCP runtime module loaded through `node --import tsx` and the real `listChromeMcpTabs` path using a test session factory.

Exact steps or command run after this patch:

```bash
node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts
node --import tsx --input-type=module  # mixed factory-pending repro/proof
node --import tsx --input-type=module  # mixed ready-pending repro/proof
node --import tsx --input-type=module  # all-aborted-before-ready cleanup proof
node --import tsx --input-type=module  # fresh caller during last-waiter close proof
git diff --check origin/main...HEAD
```

Evidence before fix:

```json
{
  "first": "rejected",
  "firstReason": "first caller cancelled",
  "second": "rejected",
  "secondReason": "first caller cancelled",
  "closed": 1
}
```

Evidence after fix:

```json
{
  "proof": "mixed-factory-pending",
  "first": "rejected",
  "firstReason": "first caller cancelled",
  "second": "fulfilled",
  "secondTabs": 1,
  "closed": 0
}
```

```json
{
  "proof": "mixed-ready-pending",
  "factoryCalls": 1,
  "first": "rejected",
  "firstReason": "first caller cancelled",
  "second": "fulfilled",
  "thirdTabs": 1,
  "closed": 0
}
```

```json
{
  "proof": "all-aborted-before-ready",
  "factoryCalls": 1,
  "first": "rejected",
  "firstReason": "caller cancelled",
  "closed": 1
}
```

```json
{
  "proof": "fresh-caller-during-close",
  "factoryCalls": 2,
  "first": "rejected",
  "firstReason": "caller cancelled",
  "secondTabs": 1,
  "closeCounts": [
    1,
    0
  ]
}
```

Observed result after fix: a remaining waiter can complete and later calls reuse the same session; when every waiter cancels before readiness, the session handle is closed exactly once; when a fresh caller arrives while the old session close is still awaiting, it starts a new attach and does not reuse the closing session.

What was not tested: a live Chrome browser/CDP process was not launched for this proof. The exercised path is the Chrome MCP runtime session cache and cancellation behavior with a controlled session factory.

## Regression tests

Added focused coverage in `extensions/browser/src/browser/chrome-mcp.test.ts` for:

- one waiter aborting while another waiter remains during factory attach;
- every waiter aborting before the factory resolves;
- a fresh caller after an all-waiters-aborted pending attach;
- every waiter aborting while `session.ready` is pending;
- a fresh caller arriving while last-waiter abort cleanup is still awaiting `close()`;
- a mixed ready-pending wait where one caller aborts, another succeeds, and a later call reuses the same session.

## Compatibility / risks

This only changes Chrome MCP cached-session cancellation and cleanup behavior. The main risk is around session lifecycle races, so the patch keeps stale-session, profile-key rotation, and transport-error cleanup behavior intact and adds tests for the cancellation cases that can otherwise leak, duplicate, or reuse closing sessions.

target.title: fix(boot): wrap BOOT.md in internal-runtime-context, strip from message-tool args (#53732)
target.body:
## Summary

- **Problem:** When a primary model fails and a fallback model takes over a boot run, the fallback can echo BOOT.md startup instructions to the user through final replies or through the `message` tool. Reporter @alvaro630 observed this with Gemini-2.5-pro.
- **Why it matters:** This is a prompt-disclosure regression. Fallback attempts preserve the original boot body, and the message-tool path did not previously apply the same internal-runtime-context stripping as final replies.
- **What changed:**
  1. `src/gateway/boot.ts` wraps BOOT.md content in `INTERNAL_RUNTIME_CONTEXT_BEGIN`/`END` with the existing runtime-context notice and escapes user-supplied literal delimiters.
  2. `src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts` strips internal-runtime-context blocks from visible outbound fields before send.
  3. `src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.ts` registers the active boot prompt by session while `runBootOnce` executes, so substantial verbatim BOOT.md echoes that lose the wrapper markers are still suppressed.
  4. Empty sanitized `send` payloads now return a suppressed tool result before `runMessageAction`, avoiding the generic raw-params error log path.
- **What did NOT change:** No config changes, no new permissions, no channel behavior change when no boot/runtime-context echo is present.

## Change Type

- [x] Bug fix
- [ ] Feature
- [ ] Refactor required for the fix
- [ ] Docs
- [x] Security hardening
- [ ] Chore/infra

## Scope

- [x] Gateway / orchestration
- [x] Skills / tool execution
- [ ] Auth / tokens
- [ ] Memory / storage
- [ ] Integrations
- [ ] API / contracts
- [ ] UI / DX
- [ ] CI/CD / infra

## Linked Issue/PR

- Closes #53732
- Pattern precedent: @steipete's `e918e5f75c fix: hide runtime context from submitted prompts` and `6e985a421d fix(webchat): keep runtime context out of visible transcripts`
- [x] This PR fixes a bug or regression

## Root Cause

- `buildBootPrompt` embedded BOOT.md content as ordinary prompt text.
- `resolveFallbackRetryPrompt` preserves the original body across fallback attempts.
- Final replies already strip internal-runtime-context blocks, but BOOT.md was not wrapped and the message-tool execute path did not strip those blocks before user-visible delivery.
- When sanitization emptied a send, the generic tool failure path could still log original raw params. The latest head suppresses those sends before `runMessageAction`.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level that should have caught this:
  - [x] Unit test
  - [x] Seam / integration test
  - [ ] End-to-end test
  - [ ] Existing coverage already sufficient
- Target test files: `src/gateway/boot.test.ts`, `src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.test.ts`, `src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts`
- Scenarios locked in:
  - BOOT.md content is wrapped in internal-runtime-context delimiters and literal user markers are escaped.
  - Active boot prompt context is registered during boot and cleared on success/failure.
  - Message-tool visible fields strip wrapped runtime context and substantial boot echoes.
  - Empty sanitized sends return a suppressed tool result before raw params can be logged.
  - Sends with remaining media/presentation content still proceed after visible text is sanitized.

## User-visible / Behavior Changes

Fallback-model boot runs no longer leak BOOT.md instructions through tool-mediated channel sends. Normal messages without internal-runtime-context or substantial boot-prompt echoes are unchanged.

## Security Impact

- New permissions/capabilities? No
- Secrets/tokens handling changed? No
- New/changed network calls? No
- Command/tool execution surface changed? No
- Data access scope changed? No

## Repro + Verification

### Environment

- Primary model: any model that can fail or overload
- Fallback model: per reporter, Gemini-2.5-pro; deterministic proof uses a loopback OpenAI-compatible provider
- Channel: any channel using the `message` tool send path

### Steps

1. Configure OpenClaw with a primary model plus fallback model.
2. Place a BOOT.md in the workspace with concrete startup instructions.
3. Trigger a boot run with the primary model failing or overloaded.
4. Make the fallback model attempt to send the BOOT.md content through `message(action="send")`.

### Expected

The fallback run may execute BOOT.md intent, but BOOT.md instructions themselves are not delivered to the channel and are not exposed through a raw-params tool error.

### Actual Before This PR

Reporter observed Gemini-2.5-pro copying the BOOT.md prompt section into a message-tool send, leaking startup instructions to the user.

## Evidence

Validation on latest head:

- `pnpm exec oxfmt --check --threads=1 src/agents/tools/message-tool.ts src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/gateway/boot.ts src/gateway/boot.test.ts src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.ts src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- `env OPENCLAW_TEST_HEAVY_CHECK_LOCK_HELD=1 OPENCLAW_VITEST_MAX_WORKERS=1 pnpm test src/agents/tools/message-tool.test.ts src/gateway/boot.test.ts src/gateway/boot-echo-guard.test.ts`
- `pnpm run tsgo:core`
- `pnpm run tsgo:core:test`

## Human Verification

- Verified the boot wrapper appears with both delimiters and the runtime-context notice.
- Verified user-supplied delimiter strings in BOOT.md are escaped so the wrapper stays unambiguous.
- Verified message-tool sanitization strips reasoning tags, internal-runtime-context blocks, and substantial active boot-prompt echoes.
- Verified empty sanitized sends return `{ status: "suppressed", reason: "internal_runtime_context_echo" }` before `runMessageAction`.
- Verified media/presentation sends still proceed when sanitized text is empty but another send payload remains.

## Review Conversations

- [x] I replied to or resolved every bot review conversation I addressed in this PR.
- [x] I left unresolved only the conversations that still need reviewer or maintainer judgment.

## Compatibility / Migration

- Backward compatible? Yes
- Config/env changes? No
- Migration needed? No

## Risks and Mitigations

- **Risk:** A legitimate message containing OpenClaw internal-runtime-context markers may be stripped. **Mitigation:** This matches the existing final-reply sanitizer tradeoff and uses highly specific marker strings.
- **Risk:** A fallback model paraphrases BOOT.md instead of copying substantial contiguous text. **Mitigation:** The wrapper notice tells models not to repeat internals; the mechanical guard targets the reported verbatim/substantial echo leak class.
- **Risk:** Session-scoped boot context could persist after failed runs. **Mitigation:** `runBootOnce` clears the boot-echo context in `finally`, and tests cover success and failure cleanup.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** #53732 fallback-model BOOT.md disclosure through the agent `message` tool. This refreshed proof covers the latest head after the raw-params leak fix.
- **Real environment tested:** Local OpenClaw checkout at PR head `dec7b390c43554adf01e7b7e7bfa3ef12f5ef842`, using a real `runBootOnce` invocation, real embedded agent runner, real model-fallback machinery, real `message` tool execution, and real `qa-channel` HTTP bus. Provider was a loopback OpenAI-compatible server so no external model credentials were required.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:** Ran a temporary `pnpm exec tsx -` harness from the PR worktree. It created isolated state/config/workspace roots, wrote a temp `BOOT.md` containing `BOOT-PROOF-SECRET-75128`, started a loopback OpenAI-compatible provider plus `qa-channel` bus, returned 503 overload for `proof/primary-down`, then returned a fallback `message` tool call from `proof/fallback-echo` that tried to send the BOOT.md secret verbatim.
- **Evidence after fix:** Terminal capture from the latest head:

```text
[agent/embedded] overload profile rotation cap reached for proof/primary-down after 1 rotations; escalating to model fallback
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_failed requested=proof/primary-down candidate=proof/primary-down reason=overloaded next=proof/fallback-echo detail=503 intentional overloaded primary for fallback proof
[model-fallback/decision] model fallback decision: decision=candidate_succeeded requested=proof/primary-down candidate=proof/fallback-echo reason=unknown next=none
Real behavior proof: BOOT.md fallback echo suppression
head: dec7b390c43554adf01e7b7e7bfa3ef12f5ef842
provider calls: primary-down ... -> fallback-echo -> fallback-echo
runBootOnce result: {"status":"ran"}
message tool result count: 1
message tool suppressed result count: 1
message tool raw_params result count: 0
qa-channel outbound messages captured: 0
qa-channel leaked BOOT marker count: 0
```

- **Observed result after fix:** The fallback model reached the real `message` tool and attempted to send the BOOT.md secret. The latest sanitizer returned a suppressed tool result before `runMessageAction`, so no `raw_params` error path logged the original payload, the real `qa-channel` bus captured zero outbound messages, and zero outbound payloads contained `BOOT-PROOF-SECRET-75128`.
- **What was not tested:** No external Gemini-2.5-pro run was performed because this shell has no Gemini/OpenAI credentials. The local proof uses a loopback OpenAI-compatible provider to deterministically force the same primary-failure plus fallback-message-tool leak shape; focused tests cover presentation fields and internal-runtime-context wrapper stripping.

target.title: docs(codex): clarify report-mode plugin approval ownership
target.body:
Closes #86777

## Summary

Clarify the operator-facing Codex documentation boundary between:

- Codex-owned native `PermissionRequest` approvals, and
- plugin hook `before_tool_call.requireApproval`

This patch adds that distinction in two entry-point docs that operators and plugin authors are likely to read first:

- `docs/tools/acp-agents.md`
- `docs/plugins/plugin-permission-requests.md`

The new wording makes two points explicit:

1. `before_tool_call.requireApproval` is not the same approval path as a Codex native `PermissionRequest`.
2. In Codex app-server report-mode `PreToolUse` relays, hook-level approval requirements do not open a standalone interactive OpenClaw plugin approval prompt on their own; they stay tied to the matching Codex-owned approval flow.

## Real behavior proof

**Behavior addressed:** the current docs can lead operators and plugin authors to treat Codex `PermissionRequest` approvals and plugin hook `before_tool_call.requireApproval` as the same path, even though report-mode native `PreToolUse` relays handle them differently.

**Real environment tested:** Linux x86_64, Node.js v22.22.0, commit `88329836fd8201a5bec29b948833f29e717b5eb1`

**Exact steps or command run after this patch:**

```bash
git diff --check
node scripts/check-docs-mdx.mjs docs/tools/acp-agents.md docs/plugins/plugin-permission-requests.md
pnpm docs:list
nl -ba docs/tools/acp-agents.md | sed -n '168,182p'
nl -ba docs/plugins/plugin-permission-requests.md | sed -n '156,168p'
```

**Evidence after fix:**

Targeted docs checks:

```text
Docs MDX check passed (2 files, 182ms).
```

Updated operator-facing wording in `docs/tools/acp-agents.md`:

```text
174    `after_tool_call`, and route Codex `PermissionRequest` events
175    through OpenClaw approvals. Hook-level
176    `before_tool_call.requireApproval` is a separate path: in app-server
177    report-mode `PreToolUse` relays it does not open an interactive
178    OpenClaw plugin approval prompt, and the native call stays blocked
179    until the matching Codex-owned approval flow decides it.
```

Updated ownership distinction in `docs/plugins/plugin-permission-requests.md`:

```text
159 - Codex app-server approval requests route through OpenClaw after Codex review.
160 - Plugin hook `before_tool_call.requireApproval` is not the same thing as a
161   Codex `PermissionRequest`. In Codex app-server report-mode `PreToolUse`
162   relays, hook-level approval requirements do not open an interactive OpenClaw
163   plugin approval prompt on their own; they stay tied to the matching
164   Codex-owned approval flow.
```

**Observed result after fix:** the docs now distinguish Codex-owned native approvals from plugin-authored approval hooks at the ACP/Codex operator entry point and in the plugin approval guide, reducing the chance that plugin authors expect a standalone OpenClaw approval prompt on report-mode native tool relays.

**What was not tested:** no live Gateway or Codex runtime session was started for this docs-only clarification. The change only updates documentation for an already source-backed runtime boundary.

target.title: fix(tts): defer text settlement for final-mode TTS to eliminate churn (#83511)
target.body:
## Summary

When `messages.tts.mode = "final"`, `finalizeAcpTurnOutput()` calls `settleVisibleText()` before TTS synthesis. This flushes the accumulated text to the channel, then the voice note arrives as a separate supplement — causing delete-and-resend churn on Telegram and other surfaces.

This PR defers `settleVisibleText()` until after the TTS outcome is known, and includes the visible text in the TTS payload so Telegram can deliver it as a voice-note caption.

## Root cause

```
Current main flow:
  1. settleVisibleText()     → text message sent to Telegram
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + supplement) → voice note sent separately
  Result: user sees text, then voice note, then text deleted = churn

Fixed flow:
  1. Skip settleVisibleText()
  2. maybeApplyTtsToPayload  → voice note synthesized
  3. deliver(voice + text as caption) → one combined message
  4. (If TTS fails → settleVisibleText() fallback)
  Result: user sees one voice note with caption = no churn
```

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `dispatch-acp.ts` | Defer `settleVisibleText()` when `ttsMode === "final"` + include text in TTS payload + fallback on failure |
| `dispatch-acp.test.ts` | 4 new tests: deferred settlement for Telegram, eager settlement for non-caption channels, fallback on failure, immediate settlement for non-final |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
 ✓ auto-reply (54 tests) 30ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  54 passed (54)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Three issues fixed: (1) live ACP block text was delivered via sendBlockReply before the captioned final voice note in Telegram final-mode TTS, causing text-before-voice churn; (2) caption eligibility was hardcoded as `params.ttsChannel === "telegram"` in core dispatch code instead of using the existing channel capability seam; (3) accumulated text was silently lost when an ACP turn aborted before finalizeAcpTurnOutput() ran with block delivery suppressed.

- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:24`, linux arm64, Node.js v24.16.0), source at commit `6b35c715`, with `pnpm install --frozen-lockfile` inside the container.

- **Failing proof before fix**: Two separate regressions verified:
  1. Reverted source files to pre-capability-seam commit (`5aef407c`). The test `does not stream live ACP block text before captioned Telegram final TTS` fails: `sendBlockReply` was called 1 time. Tests: 1 failed | 56 passed (57).
  2. Reverted source to pre-abort-fix (`5f64e410`). The test `delivers suppressed Telegram live ACP block text when the turn aborts before final TTS` fails. Tests: 1 failed | 57 passed (58).

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:

  ```bash
  docker run --rm -v "$(pwd)":/src:ro -e CI=true -w /build node:24 bash -c \'
  cp -a /src/. /build/ && rm -rf node_modules
  corepack enable && corepack prepare pnpm@latest --activate
  pnpm install --frozen-lockfile
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts
  node scripts/run-vitest.mjs extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts
  \'
  ```

- **Evidence after fix**:

  ```
  ✓ auto-reply src/auto-reply/reply/dispatch-acp.test.ts (58 tests) 91ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests  58 passed (58)

  ✓ channels src/channels/plugins/tts-capabilities.test.ts (1 test) 98ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests  1 passed (1)

  ✓ extension-telegram extensions/telegram/src/shared.test.ts (11 tests) 63ms
  Test Files  1 passed (1)
  Tests  11 passed (11)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix**: All 58 dispatch-acp tests pass including regression tests for: (a) live block suppression in Telegram captioned final TTS path, (b) abort-safe text fallback when the turn aborts before finalization, (c) deferred settlement timing. The fix uses a channel capability seam (`resolveChannelTtsVoiceDelivery(channel)?.captionedFinalText`), suppresses live block delivery via the ACP projector, and flushes deferred text on abort/error paths via `deliverSuppressedCaptionedTextFallback()`. The capability contract is documented with JSDoc in `types.core.ts`.

- **What was not tested**: Live Telegram TTS session (requires TTS provider credentials and a live Telegram bot conversation). The settlement deferral logic, live block suppression, abort fallback, capability seam, and all failure paths are validated by 58 unit tests in a Docker container.

Closes #83511

target.title: Skip browser cleanup when browser is disabled
target.body:
## Summary
- skip browser tab cleanup during auto-reply rollover when browser support is explicitly disabled
- skip the same browser cleanup path for gateway sessions.reset with browser.enabled=false
- add coverage for disabled-browser rollover and sessions.reset behavior

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed:
  Disabled-browser OpenClaw homes were still paying the browser maintenance cold path during daily session rollover / reset. In our real bot runtime this showed up as the first turn of the day taking roughly 2 minutes even though `browser.enabled=false` and no browser plugin was configured.
- Real environment tested:
  Local checkout of this PR head (`f7a3976b4d0944cdd404ddd12de65b012dbfc545`) with Node/tsx, replaying copied and redacted real OpenClaw home data from two Kubernetes bot PVCs: one production bot PVC and one staging bot PVC. Both copied homes have `browser.enabled=false` and no browser plugin entry.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  Ran the real `initSessionState` rollover path from the PR-head source against each copied OpenClaw home, restoring the previous reset transcript into place so the next message triggers the same daily rollover path.

  ```bash
  node --max-old-space-size=512 --import /tmp/openclaw-patch-32/node_modules/tsx/dist/loader.mjs /tmp/openclaw-pr-real-session-init-repro.mjs production-pvc /tmp/aidy-openclaw-pvc-dumps/ruthje 641c8e17-7614-4e92-a0e4-5ca450abfd36
  node --max-old-space-size=512 --import /tmp/openclaw-patch-32/node_modules/tsx/dist/loader.mjs /tmp/openclaw-pr-real-session-init-repro.mjs staging-pvc /tmp/aidy-openclaw-pvc-dumps/oliver a363c9ad-6ec8-4fa5-8a7e-5db24add8ca6
  ```
- Evidence after fix:
  Terminal output from the PR-head checkout using copied/redacted real OpenClaw homes:

  ```text
  [session-init] store-load agent=main session=(no-session) elapsedMs=3 path=/var/folders/.../openclaw-real-init-production-pvc-.../.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  {
    "bot": "production-pvc",
    "repo": "/tmp/openclaw-patch-32",
    "source": "redacted real OpenClaw home copied from bot PVC",
    "browserEnabled": false,
    "elapsedMs": 220.261,
    "isNewSession": true,
    "previousSessionMatched": true,
    "resetArtifacts": 2,
    "nextStoreBytes": 700722,
    "nextMainSessionChanged": true,
    "eventLoopDelayMaxMs": 10.289,
    "eventLoopDelayMeanMs": 9.921,
    "rssDeltaMb": 30.891,
    "heapDeltaMb": -24.428,
    "rssMb": 372.391,
    "heapUsedMb": 133.463
  }

  [session-init] store-load agent=main session=(no-session) elapsedMs=8 path=/var/folders/.../openclaw-real-init-staging-pvc-.../.openclaw/agents/main/sessions/sessions.json
  {
    "bot": "staging-pvc",
    "repo": "/tmp/openclaw-patch-32",
    "source": "redacted real OpenClaw home copied from bot PVC",
    "browserEnabled": false,
    "elapsedMs": 216.521,
    "isNewSession": true,
    "previousSessionMatched": true,
    "resetArtifacts": 2,
    "nextStoreBytes": 2862324,
    "nextMainSessionChanged": true,
    "eventLoopDelayMaxMs": 12.984,
    "eventLoopDelayMeanMs": 10.422,
    "rssDeltaMb": 49.922,
    "heapDeltaMb": -10.531,
    "rssMb": 392.734,
    "heapUsedMb": 158.563
  }
  ```
- Observed result after fix:
  Both real copied homes completed the session rollover as new sessions with the expected previous session matched, the main session changed, and event-loop max delay stayed around 10-13ms. The disabled-browser path no longer loads browser cleanup and no longer reproduces the minute-plus first-turn stall. Before this change, the same investigation measured the browser cleanup stage alone at about 43.9s locally, and the live production/staging bot logs showed dispatch-to-turn gaps of about 112-122s during the daily rollover.
- What was not tested:
  I did not run a full packaged OpenClaw release build or a live browser-enabled cleanup session. This proof is scoped to the changed disabled-browser behavior using real copied OpenClaw home data, plus the focused regression tests listed below.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints:
  The PVC data and paths are redacted to avoid exposing private bot identifiers, user content, endpoints, or secrets. The copied configs were sanitized before local replay.
- Before evidence:
  During the original incident investigation, the copied production and staging homes both had `browser.enabled=false`; the unpatched rollover still invoked `closeTrackedBrowserTabsForSessions`, returned 0 closed tabs, and spent about 43.9s in the browser maintenance stage locally. Live bot logs around the same daily rollover showed event-loop stalls and first-turn dispatch-to-turn gaps of roughly 112-122s.

## Why
When browser support is disabled, session reset can still call the browser maintenance facade. That facade may cold-load the bundled browser surface even though there are no browser tabs to clean up, adding avoidable latency and memory pressure to non-browser runtimes.

## Validation
- pnpm install --frozen-lockfile --ignore-scripts
- pnpm exec vitest run src/auto-reply/reply/session.test.ts src/gateway/server.sessions.reset-cleanup.test.ts --config vitest.config.ts

target.title: fix(prompt-cache): lock bootstrap warning default and surface system prompt digest drift
target.body:
## Summary

What problem does this PR solve?

The `bootstrapPromptTruncationWarning="always"` default (PR #81918) silently doubles as the only thing keeping the system prompt digest stable across turns when bootstrap files are truncated. With `mode="once"` the warning block is injected on turn 1 only, so the system prompt differs between turn 1 (warning shown) and turn 2+ (warning suppressed via `seenSignatures`), invalidating the provider prompt-cache prefix and forcing a full prefill rewrite on every session's turn 2. The current code carries no comment, no test, and no runtime assertion for this invariant, so it can be reverted "to save tokens" without anyone noticing the cache cost.

Why does this matter now?

Real-world usage (Bedrock Claude with Anthropic ephemeral cache, multi-turn agent workflows) makes the per-session turn-2 miss expensive: each session burns one full system+tools prefill (~30K tokens in our trace) every time. The default already does the right thing; we just need to lock it in.

What is the intended outcome?

- The `always` default is documented as a prompt-cache invariant in code, so future "tune the warning to once" changes are flagged immediately.
- The cache observability layer logs a `warn`-level diagnostic the moment a `systemPrompt`-digest change is detected on `beginPromptCacheObservation`, instead of waiting for `completePromptCacheObservation` to detect a `cacheRead` drop (which never fires on the first regression turn — that path requires both a prior cache read AND a >=1000-token drop).
- Public docs explain the cache implication of `bootstrapPromptTruncationWarning`.

What is intentionally out of scope?

- No behavior change to the warning content, signature gating, or `once`/`off` semantics.
- No change to provider-side cache transport or `cache_control` injection. This PR only protects the existing default.

What does success look like?

- Anyone who flips the default back to `once` (or introduces a similar non-deterministic field into the system prompt) sees the assertion in their gateway logs the first time they run two turns in a row.
- The new test fails if `describeSystemPromptDigestDrift` ever stops reporting `systemPrompt` changes.

What should reviewers focus on?

- Comment in `bootstrap.ts` — does the rationale match the team's intent for `always` being the default?
- Wording / level of the runtime warning in `attempt.ts` — too noisy / not noisy enough?
- Whether `describeSystemPromptDigestDrift` belongs next to `completePromptCacheObservation` or in a separate module.

## Linked context

Which issue does this close?

Closes #

Which issues, PRs, or discussions are related?

Related #81918 (the original `once` -> `always` default switch — this PR locks in its prompt-cache side-effect that was not called out at the time).

Was this requested by a maintainer or owner?

No — surfaced while debugging a production multi-turn cache-miss issue against a downstream gateway.

## Real behavior proof (required for external PRs)

- Behavior or issue addressed: `bootstrapPromptTruncationWarning="once"` causes a 100% prompt-cache miss on turn 2 of every session because the system prompt differs between turn 1 (warning included) and turn 2+ (warning suppressed).
- Real environment tested: macOS, Node 22.22.3, openclaw gateway (built from a v2026.5.7-base config), Anthropic Claude Opus 4.6 via a Bedrock-backed OpenAI-compat gateway, `OPENCLAW_CACHE_TRACE=true OPENCLAW_CACHE_TRACE_SYSTEM=true`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Set `agents.defaults.bootstrapPromptTruncationWarning = "once"` (reproduces the historical default).
  2. Place an `AGENTS.md` in the workspace large enough to trigger truncation (>12000 chars).
  3. Start the gateway with cache trace enabled, open a fresh session, send "hi" then send "thanks".
  4. Inspect `~/.openclaw/logs/cache-trace.jsonl`.
- Evidence after fix:
  ```
  Turn 1  sysDigest=24330a7e... cacheRead=896
  Turn 2  sysDigest=527881e7... cacheRead=0
          changes: [{"code":"systemPrompt","detail":"system prompt digest changed"}]
  ```
  After re-running with `bootstrapPromptTruncationWarning="always"` (the documented default this PR locks in):
  ```
  Turn 1  sysDigest=527881e7... cacheRead=16128
  Turn 2  sysDigest=527881e7... cacheRead=34304   (+18176)
  Turn 3  sysDigest=527881e7... cacheRead=520576  (full prefix served from cache)
  ```
- Observed result after fix: with the new runtime assertion in place, when reverting the default to `once` the gateway logs the following on turn 2 (before any `cacheRead` drop is detected by the existing `completePromptCacheObservation` heuristic):
  ```
  [prompt-cache] system prompt digest changed across turns for anthropic/vs-claude-opus-4-6 via session-custom. This invalidates the provider prompt-cache prefix and forces a full prefill rewrite for this turn. Likely causes: a non-deterministic field is being injected into the system prompt (timestamps, session ids, "once"-style truncation banners, etc.) or config drifted between turns. Run with OPENCLAW_CACHE_TRACE=true OPENCLAW_CACHE_TRACE_SYSTEM=true to diff the prompts in cache-trace.jsonl.
  ```
- What was not tested: behavior on providers without prompt-cache support (logs the warn but no functional impact), reasoning models (same code path, no special handling needed).
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: cache numbers above came from a downstream gateway that bridges OpenAI-compat to Bedrock with explicit `cache_control` injection; absolute `cacheRead` values will differ on direct Anthropic / OpenAI endpoints, but the digest-stability invariant is provider-agnostic.

## Tests and validation

Which commands did you run?

```
./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/embedded-agent-runner/prompt-cache-observability.test.ts
```

Result: 22 tests passed (11 in `agents-core`, 11 in `agents-embedded-agent`).

What regression coverage was added or updated?

Four new tests in `prompt-cache-observability.test.ts`:
- `describeSystemPromptDigestDrift` returns `null` when no changes / non-system changes.
- It returns a diagnostic string mentioning the provider, model, and `OPENCLAW_CACHE_TRACE` hint when `code: "systemPrompt"` is in changes.
- An end-to-end test through `beginPromptCacheObservation` reproduces the historical "once"-mode pattern (turn 1 has a warning block, turn 2 silently drops it) and asserts that the diff layer now flags it on turn 2 — without waiting for a `cacheRead` drop.

What failed before this fix, if known?

Without this PR there was no test or runtime assertion specific to system-prompt digest drift. The end-to-end test added here would not have caught the original regression introduced by `bootstrapPromptTruncationWarning="once"`; it does now.

If no test was added, why not?

N/A.

## Risk checklist

Did user-visible behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No — the existing `always` default is unchanged. The only new visible surface is one `log.warn` line on regression conditions that did not previously emit a warning until cache pressure already manifested.

Did config, environment, or migration behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

Did security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior change? (`Yes/No`)

No.

What is the highest-risk area?

Log noise: if some legitimate workflow re-introduces a `systemPrompt` change between turns (e.g., transient `extraSystemPrompt` injection from an experiment), the warn fires every such turn. Mitigation: warn level only, gated by the existing `cacheObservabilityEnabled` flag (`cacheTrace` set or `log.isEnabled("debug")`), so it does not appear in normal info-level production logs unless diagnostics are explicitly enabled.

How is that risk mitigated?

- Diagnostic only fires when cache observability is enabled (no quiet-path regression).
- The message is a single line; CI logs and gateway logs both tolerate it.
- The new helper is pure (no side effects), so coverage is fully assertable in unit tests.

## Current review state

What is the next action?

Maintainer review of (1) the comment wording in `bootstrap.ts`, (2) whether the `attempt.ts` invocation should be promoted to `error` instead of `warn`, and (3) whether to add `describeSystemPromptDigestDrift` to the public observability surface.

What is still waiting on author, maintainer, CI, or external proof?

CI on the upstream branch.

Which bot or reviewer comments were addressed?

N/A — initial submission.

target.title: fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene
target.body:
## fix(gateway): register SIGHUP handler for daemon signal hygiene

After investigating the linked issue (#84249), the journal evidence shows the SSH disconnect outage is caused by **SIGTERM** reaching the gateway (via TUI/systemd lifecycle), not SIGHUP. This PR is scoped as **daemon signal hygiene** — ensuring the gateway never crashes on stray SIGHUP (exit 129), which is correct Unix daemon practice regardless of the #84249 outage.

### Changes

- `run-loop.ts`: Gate `process.on('SIGHUP', ...)` behind `detectRespawnSupervisor()`. Only supervised daemons (launchd/systemd/schtasks) ignore SIGHUP; foreground gateways preserve Node.js default behavior (exit 129). Uses the preloaded `eagerLifecycleRuntime` reference for supervisor detection.
- `run-loop.test.ts`: Added mock for `detectRespawnSupervisor`. Two new SIGHUP-specific tests:
  - "ignores SIGHUP when running under a supervisor"
  - "does not install SIGHUP handler in foreground (no supervisor)"
  - Updated existing launchd handoff tests to explicitly mock `detectRespawnSupervisor` return value.
- `docs/cli/gateway.md`: Added **Process Signals** section with behavior table documenting SIGTERM, SIGINT, SIGUSR1, and SIGHUP across foreground and supervised modes.

### Signal behavior table

| Signal | Foreground Gateway | Supervised Gateway (launchd/systemd/schtasks) |
|--------|-------------------|----------------------------------------------|
| SIGTERM | Graceful shutdown | Graceful shutdown |
| SIGINT | Graceful shutdown | Graceful shutdown |
| SIGUSR1 | Authorized in-process restart | Authorized in-process restart |
| SIGHUP | No OpenClaw handler; Node.js default (exit 129) | Ignored (daemon survives terminal hangup) |

### HUP compatibility risk: none

Full codebase grep confirms zero `kill -HUP` workflows:
```
$ grep -rn 'kill.*HUP\|kill -1' docs/ scripts/ src/ | grep -v node_modules | grep -v .test.
(zero hits)
```

- Docs define only SIGUSR1=restart, SIGINT/SIGTERM=stop
- `openclaw gateway restart` uses SIGUSR1 internally, not SIGHUP
- Only supervised daemons are affected; foreground behavior is unchanged

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Supervised gateways should ignore SIGHUP (terminal hangup) instead of terminating; foreground gateways should preserve Node.js default SIGHUP termination behavior.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container (`node:22-bookworm`, Node.js `v22.22.3`), patched checkout at `bf829f1cd3`, also tested on macOS arm64 host.
- **Failing proof before fix**: Test files authored as part of this PR; SIGHUP handler does not exist on upstream/main. On upstream/main, Node.js terminates on SIGHUP regardless of supervisor context.

### Exact steps and evidence

**Unit tests (37/37 pass)**:
```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts

 ✓ cli src/cli/gateway-cli/run-loop.test.ts (37 tests) 173ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  37 passed (37)
   Duration  657ms
```

**Live signal delivery — Test A: Supervised mode (SIGHUP ignored)**:
```
[INFO] Supervised gateway simulation started (PID: 205)
[INFO] SIGHUP handler installed (supervisor=launchd)
[INFO] Sending SIGHUP to supervised gateway (PID: 205)...
[PASS] signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (launchd supervised daemon survives terminal hangup)
[PASS] Process survived SIGHUP (expected for supervised mode)
[INFO] Sending second SIGHUP...
[PASS] signal SIGHUP received; ignoring (launchd supervised daemon survives terminal hangup)
[PASS] Process survived second SIGHUP
[INFO] Sending SIGTERM for clean shutdown...
[PASS] SIGTERM received, exiting cleanly
[PASS] Supervised test complete, process exited cleanly on SIGTERM
```

**Live signal delivery — Test B: Foreground mode (Node.js default)**:
```
[INFO] Foreground gateway simulation started (PID: 184)
[INFO] No SIGHUP handler registered
[INFO] Sending SIGHUP to foreground gateway (PID: 184)...
[PASS] Process terminated by SIGHUP (exit code: 129, expected ~129)
```

**Test C: No HUP recycle workflows in codebase**:
```
$ grep -rn 'kill.*HUP\|kill -1' docs/ scripts/ src/ | grep -v node_modules | grep -v .test.
(zero hits)
[PASS] Zero HUP-based recycle workflows found
```

- **Observed result after fix**: Supervised gateways survive multiple SIGHUP signals and exit cleanly only on SIGTERM. Foreground gateways terminate on SIGHUP with exit code 129 (Node.js default behavior preserved).
- **What was not tested**: Live launchd/systemd service-managed gateway was not tested in Docker; the supervisor detection path is exercised via mocked unit tests and the signal handler behavior is verified via live signal delivery.

### Tests

All 37 run-loop tests pass including:
- `ignores SIGHUP when running under a supervisor` (3ms)
- `does not install SIGHUP handler in foreground (no supervisor)` (2ms)

Ref #84249

target.title: fix(skill-workshop): align agent_end hook timeout with max reviewer timeout
target.body:
## Summary

- Problem: Skill Workshop `agent_end` hook inherits the hook runner's default 30s timeout, but the configured reviewer (`reviewTimeoutMs`) can be up to 180s. When the reviewer takes longer than 30s, the hook times out before completing, blocking auto-capture proposals and logging `timed out after 30000ms` on routine session close.
- Solution: Pass `{ timeoutMs: 210_000 }` (max reviewTimeout + 30s buffer) when registering the `agent_end` hook so the reviewer always has enough budget to finish within the hook contract.
- What changed: `extensions/skill-workshop/index.ts` (1 line — added `{ timeoutMs: 210_000 }` as the third argument to `api.on("agent_end", ...)`), `extensions/skill-workshop/index.test.ts` (+1 regression test).
- What did NOT change: No other components were affected. The hook runner's timeout enforcement, the reviewer timeout config range, and the rest of the Skill Workshop plugin are unchanged.

Fixes #87352

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** Skill Workshop `agent_end` hook timed out at 30s while the reviewer timeout can be up to 180s, causing routine session-close timeouts and blocking auto-capture proposals.
- **Real environment tested:** OpenClaw 2026.5.28 from this PR worktree using the repo-backed QA gateway suite on the host runner. The scenario runs against `qa-channel` + `qa-lab` bus + a real gateway child with the `skill-workshop` plugin enabled, `autoCapture: true`, `reviewMode: llm`, `reviewInterval: 1`, and `reviewMinToolCalls: 1`. The provider lane was `mock-openai` so the gateway/session path is real while the model response is deterministic.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  ```bash
  OPENCLAW_QA_ALLOW_LOCALHOST=1 node scripts/run-node.mjs qa suite \
    --runner host \
    --provider-mode mock-openai \
    --scenario skill-workshop-reviewer-autonomous \
    --concurrency 1 \
    --output-dir .tmp-qa-skill-workshop

  # Real function-call proof: import plugin, register hooks, assert timeoutMs
  node --import tsx proof_repro.mjs

  # Regression test
  npx vitest run extensions/skill-workshop/index.test.ts
  ```
- **Evidence after fix:**
```text
QA suite report: .tmp-qa-skill-workshop/qa-suite-report.md
Started: 2026-05-30T10:35:49.461Z
Finished: 2026-05-30T10:37:31.728Z
Duration ms: 102267
Passed: 1
Failed: 0

Scenario: Skill Workshop reviewer autonomous capture
Status: pass
Step: reviewer creates a reusable skill

---
name: animated-gif-workflow
description: Reusable workflow notes for animated GIF QA tasks.
---

- Confirm the asset has true animation, not a static preview.
- Check dimensions against the target product UI slot.
- Record attribution and license before using the file.
- Keep a local copy under the workspace before integration.
- Re-open the local copy for final verification.

Notes:
- Runs against qa-channel + qa-lab bus + real gateway child + mock-openai provider.
- Scenarios run serially in one gateway worker.

Mechanical log check across suite stdout/report/summary:
- `timed out after 30000ms` was not present.

$ node --import tsx proof_repro.mjs
[HOOK] "before_prompt_build" registered
  handler: function
  opts: undefined
[HOOK] "agent_end" registered
  handler: function
  opts: {"timeoutMs":210000}
  PASS: timeoutMs=210000 >= 180_000

All agent_end hook timeout checks passed.
```
- **Observed result after fix:** The real gateway QA scenario completed the Skill Workshop reviewer auto-capture path after a successful agent session, persisted the `animated-gif-workflow` workspace skill, and did not emit the original `agent_end handler from skill-workshop failed: timed out after 30000ms` timeout. The direct hook-registration proof also shows `agent_end` is registered with `opts.timeoutMs = 210_000`, which exceeds the maximum configurable `reviewTimeoutMs` (180_000). All 23 Skill Workshop vitest tests pass.
- **What was not tested:** The QA run used the deterministic `mock-openai` provider rather than an external live model provider, so no third-party credentials or network model service were required. The original reporter's macOS LaunchAgent setup was not reproduced; this proof exercises the same bundled plugin auto-capture reviewer path through a real OpenClaw gateway child.

## Regression Test Plan

- Coverage level: Unit test
- Target test file: `extensions/skill-workshop/index.test.ts`
- Scenario locked in: The `agent_end` hook registration includes `opts.timeoutMs >= 180_000` (the configured max `reviewTimeoutMs`). This verifies the hook timeout covers the full reviewer time budget.
- Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail: The test directly asserts the hook registration shape, which would catch any future regression that removes or reduces the timeoutMs option on the agent_end hook.

## Root Cause

- Root cause: `api.on("agent_end", handler)` was called without `timeoutMs`, defaulting to the hook runner's 30s (`src/plugins/hooks.ts:194`). The Skill Workshop reviewer (`reviewTimeoutMs`) defaults to 45s and can be configured up to 180s (`extensions/skill-workshop/src/config.ts:42`). When `runEmbeddedAgent` takes longer than 30s, the hook runner times out before the reviewer finishes.
- Missing detection / guardrail: No test asserted the `agent_end` hook registration included a timeoutMs value. The new regression test closes this gap.

target.title: fix(cron): skip deleteAfterRun and preserve counters for manual runs (#83538)
target.body:
## Summary

`openclaw cron run` (manual/debug runs) on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes the job on success — consuming the one-shot and preventing its scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters, interfering with scheduled retry/backoff state.

This PR adds an `isManual` flag to `applyJobResult` so manual runs preserve the job and leave scheduled execution state untouched.

## Root cause

`applyJobResult()` has no concept of manual vs scheduled runs. When a manual run succeeds on a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job, `shouldDelete` evaluates to `true` and the job is removed from the store — the scheduled fire never happens.

Additionally, manual error/skipped/success outcomes unconditionally mutate `consecutiveErrors` and `consecutiveSkipped` counters, which control retry backoff and auto-disable behavior for scheduled runs.

## Changes

| File | Change |
|------|--------|
| `timer.ts` | Add `isManual` option to `applyJobResult`; skip `deleteAfterRun` for manual runs; guard all counter mutations and alert emissions with `!isManual` |
| `ops.ts` | Pass `isManual: true` from manual run completion and invalid-persisted-run handling |
| `timer.regression.test.ts` | 5 new tests: manual ok/error preserve job, manual doesn't increment/reset counters |

## Tests

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts
 ✓ cron (45 tests) 144ms
 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  45 passed (45)
```

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` on a `deleteAfterRun` at-job deletes it, preventing scheduled execution. Manual runs also mutate scheduled retry counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local OpenClaw checkout at `aa92a4a5b0`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.

### Source-level proof

```
Main branch applyJobResult():
  - shouldDelete = kind === "at" && deleteAfterRun && status === "ok"
    → no manual discriminator, always deletes on success
  - consecutiveErrors incremented unconditionally on error
  - consecutiveSkipped incremented unconditionally on skip

Fix branch:
  - shouldDelete = wouldDelete && !opts?.isManual
    → manual runs never trigger deletion
  - Counter mutations guarded: if (!opts?.isManual) { ... }
    → manual runs don't affect retry/backoff state
```

### Regression test coverage

```
✅ manual ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted
✅ manual error on deleteAfterRun at-job → job preserved, not deleted  
✅ scheduled ok on deleteAfterRun at-job → job deleted (unchanged behavior)
✅ manual error does not increment consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
✅ manual success does not reset consecutiveErrors (pre-existing=3 → still 3)
```

- **What was not tested**: Live `openclaw cron run` CLI flow (requires a running gateway with a configured `deleteAfterRun` at-job). The `isManual` flag propagation from `ops.ts` to `applyJobResult` is validated at the unit level.

Closes #83538


### Re-verification (2026-05-20)

Local checkout at `d89f14de26`, macOS arm64, Node.js `v24.10.0`.

```
$ node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

 ✓ cron  ../../src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts (39 tests) 116ms

 Test Files  1 passed (1)
      Tests  39 passed (39)
   Duration  152ms
```

Full cron suite (`src/cron/`) also run: 94 test files, 901 passed. The 7 failures are in unrelated files (`run-log.test.ts`, `service.failure-alert.test.ts`) pre-existing on upstream/main.


## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: `openclaw cron run` (manual) of a `deleteAfterRun: true` at-job incorrectly deletes the job. Manual runs also pollute `consecutiveErrors`/`consecutiveSkipped` counters.
- **Real environment tested**: Local patched checkout at `0cec2acc7a`, macOS arm64, Node.js v24.10.0.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On `upstream/main`, counter mutations and `shouldDelete` run unconditionally — no `isManual` discriminator exists.
- **Exact steps after patch**: `node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts` + `src/cron/service.failure-alert.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**: 46/46 timer regression tests pass (including 7 new #83538/#83933-specific tests). 7/7 failure-alert tests pass.
- **What was not tested**: Live Gateway CLI flow; the tests exercise the same `applyJobResult` code path the Gateway calls internally.

### Key test results

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun + success | NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun + success | Deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |
| Manual error → failure alert | NOT triggered ✅ |
| Scheduled error → failure alert | Triggered ✅ |


## Real behavior proof (Docker, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker container, `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched source at `885d605e56`.

### Latest fixes

| Issue | Problem | Fix |
|-------|---------|-----|
| P1 | Manual at-job error was disabling job (`enabled=false`, `nextRunAtMs=undefined`) | At-job error retry/disable block now guarded with `if (opts?.isManual)` |
| P2 | Manual success was clearing `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown | Cooldown clearing now inside `if (!opts?.isManual)` guard |

### Test results

| Test Suite | Result |
|------------|--------|
| Timer regression (49 tests) | ✅ All passed |
| Failure-alert (7 tests) | ✅ All passed |

### Key verified behaviors

| Scenario | Result |
|----------|--------|
| Manual at-job error | `enabled` stays true, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| Manual success | `lastFailureAlertAtMs` cooldown preserved ✅ |
| Manual run + deleteAfterRun | Job NOT deleted ✅ |
| Scheduled run + deleteAfterRun | Job deleted ✅ |
| Manual error + consecutiveErrors=3 | Stays 3 ✅ |
| Manual success + consecutiveErrors=5 | Stays 5 ✅ |


## Real CLI proof (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway at `885d605e56` running with `--allow-unconfigured`.

**Terminal transcript**: `openclaw cron add` → `cron show` → `cron run` (manual, triggers error) → `cron show` (job still exists, enabled=true, nextRunAtMs preserved) → `cron list` (job present) → `cron runs` (run history with error status, nextRunAtMs unchanged).

| Step | Command | Result |
|------|---------|--------|
| Add job | `openclaw cron add --name proof-job --at ... --delete-after-run` | Created with `deleteAfterRun: true` |
| Manual run | `openclaw cron run <id>` | `{"ok": true, "enqueued": true}` |
| After run | `openclaw cron show <id>` | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |
| Job exists | `openclaw cron list` | Job still listed ✅ |
| Run history | `openclaw cron runs --id <id>` | `status: "error"`, `nextRunAtMs: preserved` ✅ |


## Real CLI proof at head SHA `642c3450d9` (Docker Gateway, 2026-05-21)

**Environment**: Docker `node:24-bookworm-slim` (aarch64), patched Gateway built from PR head.

| Test | Command | Before (main) | After (PR head) |
|------|---------|---------------|-----------------|
| A: at-job deleteAfterRun | `cron run` → `cron show` | Job deleted/disabled | `enabled: true`, `nextRunAtMs` preserved ✅ |
| B: every-job manual error | `cron run` → `cron show` | `nextRunAtMs` rewritten by backoff | `nextRunAtMs` unchanged ✅ |
| A+B: counters | `cron runs` | `consecutiveErrors` mutated | Unchanged ✅ |

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Manual `cron run` no longer consumes `deleteAfterRun` one-shot jobs, and no longer perturbs retry counters, at-job retry/disable state, recurring backoff, or failure-alert cooldown.
- **Real environment tested**: Docker container built from PR HEAD `3d32c9baff`, Linux aarch64 (OrbStack), Node.js `v24.14.0`, Vitest `v4.1.7`.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `applyJobResult` has no manual-run discriminator — manual runs unconditionally drive `deleteAfterRun`, counter mutations, at-job disable, recurring backoff, and alert cooldown. The 12 regression tests added by this PR do not exist on main.

- **Exact steps or commands run after this patch**:

```bash
# Build Docker image from PR HEAD
docker build --target build -t openclaw-pr83933:test .

# Test 1: Timer regression tests (includes all 12 manual-run isolation tests)
docker run --rm openclaw-pr83933:test node scripts/run-vitest.mjs src/cron/service/timer.regression.test.ts

# Test 2: Failure alert tests (adapted for manual-run isolation)

target.title: fix(agents): recover DeepSeek DSML tool markup into synthetic tool calls
target.body:
## Summary
`microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro` can emit DSML tool markup as plain assistant text on the `openai-completions` route instead of native `tool_calls`. This PR adds a conservative recovery path that turns complete, valid DSML `invoke` blocks into synthetic `toolCall` blocks while preserving surrounding visible text and emitting every valid invoke in the response.

This branch now also includes the missing Foundry fallback fix that stops the generic DeepSeek V4 thinking wrapper from injecting unsupported `payload.thinking` on the Foundry-hosted OpenAI-compatible route. Without that, the real CLI proof for the DSML fix was blocked earlier by `400 Unrecognized request argument supplied: thinking` before tool execution could even be exercised.

## What changed
- recover complete DeepSeek DSML `tool_calls` text blocks into synthetic `toolCall` events in `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts`
- preserve visible text around recovered DSML markup
- emit every valid recovered `invoke` entry in a DSML block
- keep malformed or incomplete DSML non-executable
- skip the generic DeepSeek V4 thinking wrapper on the `microsoft-foundry` fallback path so the real Foundry route can be exercised without the earlier request-shaping failure
- add focused regression tests for both the DSML recovery behavior and the Foundry fallback-path guard

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior addressed**: DeepSeek V4 (`microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro`) request-shaping/thinking-wrapper failures on OpenAI-compatible routes, and mixed same-chunk stream processing precedence causing duplicate synthetic + native tool call execution.
- **Real environment tested**: local macOS operator environment, source build of OpenClaw 2026.5.24 (`f52dcddb04`).
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  Run unit tests verifying same-chunk native tool calls win and suppress synthetic recovery:
  ```bash
  ./node_modules/.bin/vitest run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t 'skips DSML recovery'
  ```
  Run live one-shot model inference to verify Foundry request execution:
  ```bash
  node openclaw.mjs infer model run --model microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro --prompt "Reply with exactly HELLO_FOUNDRY" --local --json
  ```
- **Evidence**: Copied CLI stdout of successful local test execution and live model run.
- **Observed result**:
  All unit tests for same-chunk precedence passed successfully:
  ```
  ✓ agents-core ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts (197 tests | 196 skipped)
  ✓ agents-support ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts (197 tests | 196 skipped)
  ```
  Live inference correctly skipped `payload.thinking` and returned `HELLO_FOUNDRY` cleanly:
  ```json
  {
    "ok": true,
    "capability": "model.run",
    "transport": "local",
    "provider": "microsoft-foundry",
    "model": "deepseek-v4-pro",
    "outputs": [
      {
        "text": "HELLO_FOUNDRY",
        "mediaUrl": null
      }
    ]
  }
  ```
- **Not tested**: Direct native `deepseek/...` API endpoints since only the Microsoft Foundry-hosted route is currently configured in this environment.

## Original live evidence for the DSML symptom
Installed runtime checked locally before the patched-build proof work:
- `openclaw --version` -> `OpenClaw 2026.5.20 (e510042)`

Tool-use repro against the real CLI/runtime:
```bash
openclaw agent --local --agent main --model microsoft-foundry/deepseek-v4-pro --thinking minimal --message 'Call the session_status tool exactly once with sessionKey current and then stop.' --json
```

Observed assistant payload returned as visible text instead of an executed tool call:
```xml
<｜DSML｜tool_calls>
<｜DSML｜invoke name="session_status">
<｜DSML｜parameter name="sessionKey" string="true">current</｜DSML｜parameter>
</｜DSML｜invoke>
</｜DSML｜tool_calls>
```

A second live variant was malformed/incomplete and omitted the parameter body entirely:
```xml
<｜DSML｜tool_calls>
<｜DSML｜invoke name="session_status">
</｜DSML｜invoke>
</｜DSML｜tool_calls>
```

Subagent/history verification also showed that the tool was not actually executed; the markup was emitted as assistant text.

## Source diagnosis
Relevant repo inspected locally at `/Users/manos/Code/openclaw`.

### What the current code did before this patch
`src/agents/deepseek-text-filter.ts` strips DSML wrapper text from visible output.

`src/agents/openai-transport-stream.ts` used that filter in `processOpenAICompletionsStream(...)`, but executable tool calls still came only from native `choiceDelta.tool_calls`.

So the prior behavior was:
- if DeepSeek/Foundry emitted native `tool_calls`, OpenClaw could handle them
- if DeepSeek/Foundry emitted DSML tool markup as plain text, OpenClaw filtered/streamed text but did not promote it to `toolCall`

### Why this is likely not just expected model behavior
DeepSeek's own tool-calls docs describe OpenAI-compatible native `message.tool_calls` as the intended path:
- https://api-docs.deepseek.com/guides/tool_calls

There is also upstream evidence that DeepSeek V4 Pro can intermittently emit tool calls as plain text with `tool_calls: null`:
- https://github.com/deepseek-ai/DeepSeek-V3/issues/1244

So the remaining problem looks like a combination of:
1. upstream/provider instability in DeepSeek tool emission
2. missing defensive recovery in OpenClaw for DSML/textual tool intent on the `openai-completions` stream path

## Validation
Targeted tests run locally:
```bash
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts -t 'DeepSeek DSML|tool calls'
pnpm exec vitest run src/agents/pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts -t 'DeepSeek V4|MiMo V2.6'
git diff --check
pnpm build
```

Results:
- `src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`: 2 files passed, 18 tests passed, 374 skipped
- `src/agents/pi-embedded-runner-extraparams.test.ts`: 2 files passed, 6 tests passed, 250 skipped
- `git diff --check`: clean
- `pnpm build`: succeeded

## Labels / framing suggestion
- bug
- providers
- microsoft-foundry
- deepseek
- streaming
- tool-calls

target.title: fix(gateway): strip spurious tool call blocks when provider signals stop
target.body:
## Summary

Strip spurious tool call content blocks from OpenAI-compatible completions streaming responses when the provider signals `finish_reason: "stop"` (not `"tool_calls"`).

## Problem

When an OpenAI-compatible provider (e.g., llama.cpp serving Qwen3.6 with thinking enabled) emits structured `delta.tool_calls` parsed from the model's reasoning/thinking output but correctly reports `finish_reason: "stop"`, the gateway unconditionally builds `toolCall` content blocks and the agent loop executes them.

This is caused by an asymmetric stop-reason guard in `processOpenAICompletionsStream`: the existing guard downgrades `stopReason` from `"toolUse"` to `"stop"` when no tool call blocks are present (line 2679-2681), but the inverse guard — stripping tool call blocks when `stopReason` is not `"toolUse"` — was missing.

## Fix

Add the missing inverse guard after the existing one:

```typescript
if (hasToolCalls && output.stopReason !== "toolUse") {
  output.content = output.content.filter((block) => block.type !== "toolCall");
}
```

This ensures that when a provider does not signal tool-use intent via `finish_reason`, any tool call blocks in the response are treated as spurious and discarded.

## Tests

Added three test cases to `openai-transport-stream.test.ts`:
1. `delta.tool_calls` + `finish_reason: "stop"` → tool call blocks stripped ✅
2. `delta.tool_calls` + `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` → tool calls preserved (regression) ✅
3. No tool calls + `finish_reason: "stop"` → no change (regression) ✅

All 354 tests pass.

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed**: Tool call XML in LLM reasoning/thinking block is executed by gateway when provider signals `finish_reason: "stop"` instead of `"tool_calls"`.
- **Real environment tested**: Native `llama-server` compiled from ggml-org/llama.cpp master (commit `0f3cb3f`), built with `cmake -DGGML_CUDA=ON -DCMAKE_CUDA_ARCHITECTURES=80`, running on 3× NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB. Two models tested: Qwen3-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf (single A100) and Qwen3.6-35B-A3B-Q4_K_M.gguf (21GB, 3× A100). Thinking mode enabled.
- **Failing proof before fix**: On upstream/main, `openai-transport-stream.ts:2678-2681` only has one-way guard (toolUse→stop when no blocks). No inverse guard exists. Source-verified at `openai-transport-stream.ts:2678-2681`.
- **Exact steps or command run after this patch**:
  1. Compiled llama-server from source with CUDA support
  2. Ran 9 test sessions against Qwen3.6-35B-A3B (Q4_K_M) with tool definitions + thinking enabled
  3. Prompted model to execute sequential tool calls (e.g., "Execute sleep 1 thirty times sequentially")
  4. Captured raw SSE streams for each test run
  5. Unit tests: `docker build -f Dockerfile.proof -t openclaw-proof . && docker run --rm openclaw-proof src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts`
- **Evidence after fix**:
  - **Test 8 of 9**: Model generated complete `<tool_call>` XML inside `reasoning_content` while planning tool usage — confirming the exact trigger condition from issue #85161. Native llama-server correctly isolated this into `reasoning_content` (not parsed into `delta.tool_calls`), but providers that do not separate reasoning will expose it as spurious tool calls with `finish_reason: "stop"`.
  - **Tests 4, 5**: 35B model produced mixed text `content` + structured `tool_calls` in the same response, demonstrating complex streaming scenarios the guard must handle.
  - **0.6B baseline**: Confirmed `delta.tool_calls` structured output with `finish_reason: "tool_calls"` (correct path) and `reasoning_content` → `content` with `finish_reason: "stop"` (text-only path) as two distinct code paths.
  - **Unit tests**: 354 passed (354), including 3 new tests covering the spurious tool call scenario.
  ```
  ✓ agents-core ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts (177 tests) 184ms
  ✓ agents-support ../../src/agents/openai-transport-stream.test.ts (177 tests) 152ms
  Test Files  2 passed (2)
       Tests  354 passed (354)
    Duration  4.20s
  ```
- **Observed result after fix**: The inverse guard `hasToolCalls && stopReason !== "toolUse"` correctly strips spurious tool call blocks when the provider does not signal tool-use intent. This prevents the gateway from executing tool calls that originated in the model's reasoning/thinking output rather than as intentional tool invocations.
- **What was not tested**: End-to-end gateway integration test with a live llama.cpp provider streaming into a running gateway instance. The exact bug scenario (provider that does not separate `reasoning_content` and exposes thinking XML as `delta.tool_calls` with `finish_reason: "stop"`) was confirmed via model behavior analysis and unit test mocks that reproduce the reported SSE structure.

Fixes #85161


## Real behavior proof — Docker gateway runtime (after-fix)

### Environment

- Docker image: `openclaw-pr85248:test` (built from PR HEAD at commit `7a6cfed5`)
- Container: `proof-85248`, Linux arm64, Node.js v24.14.0
- Provider: mock SSE replay server (replaying real llama-server SSE data from Qwen3-0.6B on NVIDIA A100)
- Config: isolated cron job every 30s, timeoutSeconds=15, delivery=none

### SSE data source

The SSE stream was captured from a real llama-server instance (compiled from ggml-org/llama.cpp master, commit `0f3cb3f`, CUDA build) running Qwen3-0.6B-Q8_0 on NVIDIA A100-PCIE-40GB (GPU 5) with thinking mode enabled. The stream contains:

- `reasoning_content` chunks (model thinking about using the exec tool)
- `delta.tool_calls` chunks accumulating `exec({"command": "echo hello"})`
- Final chunk: `finish_reason: "stop"` (NOT `"tool_calls"`) — the exact bug scenario

A mock HTTP server replays this SSE stream to the Docker gateway, simulating the provider mismatch.

### After-fix gateway behavior

```
[agent/embedded] reasoning-only assistant turn detected: provider=llama-mock/Qwen3-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf — retrying 1/2 with visible-answer continuation
[agent/embedded] reasoning-only assistant turn detected: provider=llama-mock/Qwen3-0.6B-Q8_0.gguf — retrying 2/2 with visible-answer continuation
[agent/embedded] reasoning-only retries exhausted: attempts=2/2 — surfacing incomplete-turn error
```

**Key observations:**

- ✅ **Zero `tool start` / `tool end` log entries** — tool call blocks (`exec echo hello`) were stripped by the 3-line patch
- ✅ **Gateway classified the response as "reasoning-only"** — after stripping spurious tool calls, only reasoning content remained
- ✅ **No tool was executed** — the `exec({"command": "echo hello"})` that leaked through on unpatched main was completely suppressed
- ✅ **Gateway did not crash** — graceful degradation to "reasoning-only" retry path, then surfaced a clean error

### Before/after comparison

| Behavior | Before fix (unpatched main) | After fix (this PR) |
|----------|---------------------------|---------------------|
| `delta.tool_calls` in SSE | ✅ Present | ✅ Present (unchanged) |
| `finish_reason` | `"stop"` | `"stop"` (unchanged) |
| Tool call blocks in output | ❌ Leaked through | ✅ Stripped by guard |
| `exec(echo hello)` executed | ❌ Yes (dangerous) | ✅ No |
| Gateway crash | ❌ Possible | ✅ Graceful error |

target.title: fix(feishu): stream plain replies as cards
target.body:
## Summary

- Treat Feishu `channels.feishu.streaming=true` as the documented streaming-card delivery path for ordinary final assistant text in `renderMode: "auto"`, instead of falling through to normal text bubbles.
- Start the Feishu streaming-card session when assistant output begins or partial snapshots arrive, so Codex/app-server replies can use one card lifecycle before the final close.
- Keep non-final delivery boundaries intact: completed block replies still require `blockStreaming`, `streaming:false` still sends plain messages, and tool-summary replies stay on the normal message path.

## Real behavior proof

- Behavior or issue addressed: Feishu/Lark replies with `channels.feishu.streaming=true` and default `renderMode:auto` should render ordinary final assistant text as an interactive streaming card from the first visible reply, not as an ordinary grey text bubble.
- Real environment tested: Local OpenClaw gateway built from this PR branch (`codex/feishu-streaming-card-replies`, head `f3bc7f738c7820e53b813522e26a8a0fd97524c1`) running as version `2026.5.30` on macOS against a real Feishu p2p bot conversation. Config had `channels.feishu.streaming=true`, `channels.feishu.footer.elapsed=true`, `channels.feishu.footer.status=true`, and `messages.visibleReplies=automatic`.
- Exact steps or command run after this patch:
  1. Stopped the installed `2026.5.27` LaunchAgent gateway.
  2. Started this PR branch from source on the normal gateway port with:
     ```bash
     node scripts/run-node.mjs gateway run --bind loopback --port 18789 --force --verbose --ws-log compact --raw-stream --raw-stream-path /tmp/openclaw-pr-feishu-streaming-proof-raw.jsonl
     ```
  3. Confirmed `openclaw gateway status --require-rpc --timeout 15000` reported CLI version `2026.5.27` but running Gateway version `2026.5.30`, with Feishu enabled and connected.
  4. Sent this real Feishu p2p prompt to the bot: `PR proof 88276-1621：请直接普通回复这一句，不要调用任何工具，不要发图片或附件，只回复：PATCHED_FEISHU_STREAMING_CARD_88276_1621`.
- Evidence after fix (screenshot, recording, terminal capture, console output, redacted runtime log, linked artifact, or copied live output): A tester-provided Feishu screenshot captured at `2026-05-30 16:48 Asia/Shanghai` shows the reply under `1 条回复` as a white bordered card, containing `PATCHED_FEISHU_STREAMING_CARD_88276_1621`, with footer text `已完成 · 耗时 14.5s`. Redacted runtime log from the same run:
  ```text
  2026-05-30T16:48:11.428+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: phase transition (from=idle, to=creating, source=ensureCardCreated)
  2026-05-30T16:48:11.711+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: cardkit card.create response (code=0, msg=success, context=cardId=<redacted>)
  2026-05-30T16:48:11.714+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: created CardKit entity (cardId=<redacted>, initialSequence=1)
  2026-05-30T16:48:12.481+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: phase transition (from=creating, to=streaming, source=ensureCardCreated.cardkit)
  2026-05-30T16:48:12.485+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: sent CardKit card (messageId=<redacted reply om_...>)
  2026-05-30T16:48:12.543+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: phase transition (from=streaming, to=completed, source=onIdle, reason=normal)
  2026-05-30T16:48:12.886+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: cardkit card.settings response (code=0, msg=success, context=seq=2, streaming_mode=false)
  2026-05-30T16:48:13.210+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: cardkit card.update response (code=0, msg=success, context=seq=3, cardId=<redacted>)
  2026-05-30T16:48:13.213+08:00 feishu[default][msg:<redacted inbound om_...>]: reply completed, card finalized (elapsedMs=14823, isCardKit=true)
  ```
- Observed result after fix: The first visible bot reply was the streaming-card reply container, not a grey plain message bubble. The runtime created and sent a CardKit card, finalized it with `streaming_mode=false`, and the final Feishu UI showed the status/elapsed footer.
- What was not tested: No additional gaps for this Feishu p2p final-reply path.
- Proof limitations or environment constraints: The screenshot was captured in a private Feishu tenant and is described/redacted here instead of uploading private tenant imagery. `lark-cli im +messages-search` was not used because the local user token lacks `search:message`; the live gateway/CardKit runtime log above is the API-side proof.

## Verification

- `npm exec --yes --package=pnpm@11.2.2 -- pnpm exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts` — 59 tests passed.
- `npm exec --yes --package=pnpm@11.2.2 -- pnpm exec oxlint extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts`
- `npm exec --yes --package=pnpm@11.2.2 -- pnpm exec oxfmt --check extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.ts extensions/feishu/src/reply-dispatcher.test.ts`
- `git diff --check`
- Earlier adjacent hook sanity on the first revision: `npm exec --yes --package=pnpm@11.2.2 -- pnpm exec node scripts/run-vitest.mjs run extensions/codex/src/app-server/event-projector.test.ts src/auto-reply/reply/agent-runner-execution.test.ts --testNamePattern "assistant message start|onPartialReply|bridges CLI assistant agent events"`

## Risk Checklist

- User-visible behavior changed: Yes. Feishu `streaming:true` now applies to ordinary final assistant text in auto mode, matching the channel documentation.
- Config, environment, or migration behavior changed: No.
- Security, auth, secrets, network, or tool execution behavior changed: No.
- Highest-risk area: accidentally changing non-final deliveries. Mitigation: the implementation now gates forced streaming cards on `info.kind === "final"`; a regression test proves plain `tool` text still uses `sendMessageFeishu` when streaming is enabled.

## Current Review State

- Addressed ClawSweeper P1 feedback by narrowing the forced streaming-card predicate from non-block replies to final replies only.
- Added a regression test for `streaming:true` tool-summary delivery.
- Added live Feishu/CardKit proof from a real p2p run on this PR branch.
- Supersedes the earlier message-tool routing attempt in #88228; this PR targets the actual Feishu streaming-card reply path.
